kept.text:
The California Coastal Commission has issued a formal apology to @elonmusk and SpaceX, adding that it will not consider political views or speech in future regulatory decisions.

• The Commission admits some officials made politically biased and improper statements about SpaceX and Elon Musk
• It formally apologizes for those remarks
• Agrees it will not consider political views or speech in future regulatory decisions
• The Commission will NOT require coastal permits for most launch operations at Vandenberg

Full statement: "The Commission agrees that it may not consider irrelevant factors in performing its function and specifically agrees that it will not take into account the perceived political beliefs, political speech, or labor practices of SpaceX or its officers in considering any regulatory action concerning SpaceX. The Commission acknowledges that Commissioners made statements, including during their October 10, 2024, hearing on the Base’s Falcon 9 launch program, that showed political bias against SpaceX and its CEO and were improper. The Commission apologizes for those statements, as set forth in the signed letter attached as Exhibit C."

This filing means that SpaceX and the California Coastal Commission have reached a settlement in their federal lawsuit, and it provides long-term regulatory clarity for SpaceX for future Falcon 9 launches.

SpaceX has tied Elon Musk’s pay to bold goals like building a Mars colony and space-based data centers. He could earn massive share awards if the company hits a $7.5 trillion valuation and establishes a million-person presence on Mars, along with major computing capacity in orbit.

He gets nothing unless these targets are met and currently earns only a minimal salary. The plan is highly unusual and may create tension with Tesla, as both companies compete for his attention.

kept.text:
Halter announced today the launch of direct-to-satellite connectivity using SpaceX's @Starlink for its smart cattle collars, a world-first that removes the need for cell towers or on-ranch infrastructure.

"Using Starlink enables ranchers to manage cattle anywhere they can see the sky. Combined with a suite of new tools for reproduction, animal behavior, and precision pasture management, the release significantly expands what is possible for cattle ranch management. 

Beef ranchers in remote and rugged regions that were limited by connectivity can now turn to virtual fencing to run more productive and sustainable operations - at a time when they face rising fuel costs, labor shortages, and an aging workforce pressures."

Halter’s internal modeling estimates direct-to-satellite capability expands coverage of the U.S beef cattle market by 2.5x. Until now, Halter’s solar-powered, GPS-enabled collars relied on Halter’s proprietary long-range radio towers. With direct-to-satellite, the collars can communicate via Starlink, eliminating ground infrastructure entirely.

kept.text:
(2/10) In 2022, SpaceX gave the Pentagon an ultimatum: pay roughly $400 million a year for Starlink service in Ukraine—crucial infrastructure the country's military was relying on for battlefield communication—or it would be cut off. 

Colin Kahl, then Under-Secretary of Defense for Policy, called Elon Musk and pleaded with for more time. Officials told me lives hung in the balance. But they had to be deferential. "Even though Musk is not technically a diplomat or statesman, I felt it was important to treat him as such, given the influence he had on this issue," Kahl told me. A Pentagon official described the dynamic more bluntly: "We are living off his good graces. That sucks."

kept.text:
(3/10) The stakes were not theoretical. Ukrainian forces advancing into contested territory in the south found their Starlink connections suddenly severed—American and Ukrainian officials told me they believed SpaceX had cut access via geofencing.

"Communications became dead, units were isolated," a signal-corps soldier told me. "Commanders had to drive to the battlefield to be in radio range, risking themselves. It was chaos." (A @Reuters investigation last year by @joroulette, @CassellBryanLow and @BalmforthTom also found that Musk personally ordered a Starlink shutdown during a Ukrainian counteroffensive.)

On the call with Kahl, Musk volunteered that he had spoken with Putin personally. (Musk later denied discussing Ukraine with Putin.) Weeks earlier, at a conference in Aspen, Musk had appeared onstage to suggest the U.S. should negotiate peace with Putin. Reid Hoffman, who helped start PayPal with Musk, told me Musk seemed to have "bought what Putin was selling, hook, line, and sinker."

kept.text:
(6/10) Regulators who tried to enforce against Musk's companies described a similar pattern.

In December 2020, the FAA explicitly told SpaceX it was not cleared to launch a Starship prototype. SpaceX launched anyway. The rocket exploded on landing. In a series of letters, the FAA's space division accused SpaceX of "a concerning lack of operational control and process discipline that is inconsistent with a strong safety culture."

Wayne Monteith, then head of the FAA's space division, told me a fine wouldn't have mattered: "He could pull that out of his pocket."

profile_bio.description: 🚀 Spaceflight Photographer and Content Manager for @SuperclusterHQ

kept.text:
Starlink: Growth, Scale, and What Comes Next

As of April 2026, Starlink has ~11.8 million subscribers across 150+ countries and is already the profit engine inside SpaceX. 2025 revenue came in around $11B. 

And it’s still accelerating. 🚀

Estimates point to ~$18–20B in 2026 and up to ~20 million users by year end.

Where this goes next
Over the next 5 years, Starlink has a real path to 40–60+ million subscribers and ~$30–40B+ in annual revenue—driven by global demand across residential, aviation, maritime, enterprise, and government.

Up next: Direct-to-Cell 📱
No dish. Just your phone.

Starlink is pushing connectivity straight to standard smartphones via carrier partnerships while positioning itself to become a global carrier. If this scales, every phone becomes a potential endpoint, expanding far beyond traditional broadband.

Starlink isn’t just Internet service anymore— it’s becoming core global infrastructure. High-margin, recurring revenue. Expanding use cases. Massive surface area for growth.

And Starlink will be part of every Tesla robot, the ones with wheels and the ones with legs. 

Still early. Still ramping.

kept.text:
SpaceX’s board approved a striking compensation plan for Elon Musk in January, directly linking his rewards to the company’s most audacious targets. Under the deal, revealed in @SpaceX’s confidential SEC filing ahead of its planned June IPO, @elonmusk stands to receive 200 million super-voting restricted shares if the firm reaches a 7.5 trillion dollar market value and establishes a permanent human colony on Mars with at least one million residents. 

He could earn up to 60.4 million more shares by meeting separate valuation hurdles and operating space-based data centers delivering 100 terawatts of compute capacity. Both awards carry 10-to-1 voting power and vest in stages as milestones are hit. 

Musk continues to draw only a nominal 54,080 dollar annual salary. The structure keeps his incentives aligned with SpaceX’s push toward multiplanetary life, even as the company targets a 1.75 trillion dollar valuation at its upcoming public debut. 

It is a pay package written for the long game.

kept.text:
Filtronic got punished yesterday. 

Why? Probably because of the customer dependence. Around 75% of the revenue comes from SpaceX. 

Investors saw what such a customer dependence can do with $POET yesterday. If SpaceX pulls out, the business case of Filtronic looks a lot less positive. 

But here is why I think Filtronic is different, very different. 

First of all, SpaceX is open about the relationship with Filtronic. They already called them a ‘reliable partner’. 

Secondly, SpaceX isn’t just a customer, it’s a strategic partner. They work together to innovate the whole space industry. 

Thirdly, Filtronic makes profit. They have a positive cash flow and ebitda margin. They don’t burn any cash and SpaceX is financing their whole R&D project. 

Finally, the management has always been honest and open about their targets and reached them. I’ve build a framework that rated management and they score a 96/100. The highest score I’ve seen until now. Poet scores 19/100. 

If the drawback continues and investors dare to push this share below 200, I’m ready to make Filtronic my second highest holding.

kept.text:
머스크의 보상, 화성 식민지와 우주 데이터센터 목표에 연동

SpaceX는 일론 머스크의 보상을 화성 식민지 건설과 우주 기반 데이터센터 구축 같은 대담한 목표에 연동했다.

회사가 기업가치 7조 5천억 달러에 도달하고, 화성에 100만 명 규모의 거주지를 구축하며, 궤도상에 대규모 컴퓨팅 인프라를 마련할 경우 머스크는 막대한 규모의 주식 보상을 받을 수 있다.

이 목표들이 달성되지 않으면 그는 아무 보상도 받지 못하며, 현재는 최소한의 급여만 받고 있다.

이러한 보상 구조는 매우 이례적이며, 머스크의 시간과 집중을 두고 Tesla와의 긴장을 유발할 가능성도 있다.

quoted_tweet.text:
MUSK PAY TIED TO MARS COLONY AND SPACE DATA CENTERS

SpaceX has tied Elon Musk’s pay to bold goals like building a Mars colony and space-based data centers. He could earn massive share awards if the company hits a $7.5 trillion valuation and establishes a million-person presence on Mars, along with major computing capacity in orbit.

He gets nothing unless these targets are met and currently earns only a minimal salary. The plan is highly unusual and may create tension with Tesla, as both companies compete for his attention.

kept.text:
I have no doubt that SpaceX can achieve a valuation of $7.5 trillion, but sending a million people to Mars is out of the question for at least the next 100 years due to health issues. 

We simply lack the technology to keep people healthy and alive on such a journey. Reproduction also poses a serious problem, as the offspring would most likely not be viable either.

The journey itself isn't the problem, but ensuring their survival and health. Given our current understanding of the human body in space, and of space and Mars in general, this is from all we know impossible. They would suffer terribly and regret ever having embarked on this journey. And I mean every single one of them. The list of sesames we know will appear is massively long and most of them won't go away after returning to earth.

After a year of in-depth research on this project, one thing is clear: It's highly unlikely that a single person will return to Earth after a Mars mission healthy—if they return at all, and certainly not without serious, potentially fatal health problems or a drastically reduced life expectancy.

The list of known diseases is so long that undertaking such a mission would be almost insane, especially when you consider the unknown diseases that needs to be accounted for. I don't say this lightly, but almost everyone who arrives on Mars will do everything in their power to return as quickly as possible.

And to all those who now claim we have experience with humans on space stations: No, we don't. Low Earth orbit has nothing to do with the conditions the human body is exposed to during a Mars mission and the time spent there. Even long duration stay on a future station on the Moon won't give us what happens to the human body on a trip and stay on Mars.

I don't say this lightly, but Elon will never see his shares award if a million people on Mars is a KPI.

kept.text:
🚨 SpaceX ties Musk compensation to Mars colonization goal

SpaceX’s board has approved a new compensation plan for Elon Musk that directly ties major rewards to the achievement of a permanent human colony on Mars housing at least one million people, along with a company valuation of US$7.5 trillion.

The package includes up to 200 million super-voting restricted shares for hitting those milestones, plus another up to 60.4 million shares linked to operating data centers in space capable of delivering 100 terawatts of compute capacity. 

Both awards vest in tranches as valuation targets are met and require Musk’s continued employment.

This structure reflects SpaceX’s core mission to make humanity a multi-planetary species, building on the company’s aggressive timeline for Starship development and Mars missions.

profile_bio.description:
24/7 Breaking News & maximum truth on Elon Musk, X Corp & Ecosystem 🔥

Musk Economy Hub 🚀

Tesla • SpaceX • xAI • X • Starlink • Optimus • Neuralink • Boring

quoted_tweet.text:
The drive to establish a presence on Mars goes far beyond scientific curiosity - it represents humanity's best shot at long-term survival as a multi-planet species.

In a recent reflection, Elon put it plainly: 

"the reason I want to go to Mars is because I think that's the best place where humanity can become a multi-planet species and space-bearing civilization." 

He went on to describe it as "the most exciting place that we could go in the solar system."

This vision frames Mars not merely as a new frontier but as the essential next chapter - one where our species moves from being confined to a single world to thriving across the solar system. 

The challenges are immense, yet the payoff is a resilient, spacefaring future that safeguards humanity against Earth-bound risks. 

By focusing efforts here, we accelerate the technologies and mindset needed to expand outward and secure our place among the stars.

kept.text:
Max Levchin said this about his own employees at PayPal.

The people who built that company went on to found YouTube, LinkedIn, Palantir, Yelp, and SpaceX. 

Elon Musk, Peter Thiel, Reid Hoffman, Steve Chen... All of them treated PayPal as a last job before building something of their own.

Levchin watched it happen from the inside. Then he did it himself: Slide, Yelp, Affirm.

The best employees aren't the ones who are grateful to have a job. 

They're the ones who are studying everything around them because they're already planning what comes next.

kept.text:
🚨 SpaceX scrubs Falcon Heavy launch at last second due to bad weather

SpaceX was forced to stand down from today’s Falcon Heavy launch of the ViaSat-3 F3 satellite just seconds before liftoff. 

The countdown halted automatically at T-23 seconds when dark clouds and rain over Florida’s Space Coast violated launch weather criteria. 

Fueling had already commenced, making a safe pause impossible and resulting in a full scrub.

This was the first Falcon Heavy mission in more than 18 months. 

The heavy-lift rocket was carrying the final satellite in the ViaSat-3 constellation to geostationary orbit from Kennedy Space Center’s Launch Complex 39A. 

The two side boosters were scheduled for synchronized landings at Cape Canaveral while the center core would be expended.

SpaceX confirmed the vehicle and payload remain healthy. 

A new target is now set for Wednesday, April 29, with the 85-minute launch window opening at 10:13 a.m. ET.

quoted_tweet.text:
🚨 SpaceX’s triple-rocket that launched a Tesla into space is back on a mission

SpaceX’s Falcon Heavy is returning to flight after an 18-month absence, carrying the ViaSat-3 F3 satellite to geostationary orbit more than 22,000 miles above Earth.

This 12th mission for the heavy-lift rocket will depart from Launch Complex 39A at Kennedy Space Center. 

Falcon HeavyThe two side boosters are targeted for simultaneous landings at Landing Zone 2 and the newer Landing Zone 40 at Cape Canaveral Space Force Station, while the center core will be expended to deliver the heavy payload.

Following separation, the ViaSat-3 F3 satellite will undergo several months of orbit raising before entering service in late summer 2026, expanding high-capacity broadband coverage worldwide.

The flight underscores SpaceX’s dominant role in both commercial and government heavy-lift operations, building on its record of 165 launches in 2025 and nearly 85 percent of all global orbital missions.
